title: Cat Eye Discharge Guide: What Green Mucus Means for Your Pet
meta_desc: Discover why your cat has green eye mucus. Learn about infection risks, urgency levels, and why a photo helps your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
What is Green Mucus in a Cat's Eye?

Green mucus, also known as purulent discharge, is a common sign of a secondary bacterial infection. It occurs when the immune system sends white blood cells to fight off invaders like bacteria, viruses, or foreign bodies. In cats, this is frequently associated with Feline Upper Respiratory Infection (URI) or damage to the surface of the eye, such as a scratch or corneal ulcer.

Urgency Level: Medium

If you see green discharge, the urgency level is Medium. This is not usually a 'wait and see' situation because eye issues can become painful and cause permanent damage very quickly. You should aim to have your cat evaluated by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If your cat is also squinting, pawing at the eye, or if the eye surface looks cloudy, you should seek care as soon as possible.

The Importance of a Photo for Triage

Providing a high-quality photo of the affected eye can significantly assist in the triage process. A clear image helps veterinary professionals distinguish between simple discharge and more serious structural changes, such as a protruding third eyelid or visible trauma to the cornea. It also serves as a vital baseline to monitor if the condition improves or worsens while you wait for your appointment.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops for my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or over-the-counter eye drops on cats without veterinary advice. Some ingredients can be toxic to cats or may exacerbate an underlying condition like a corneal ulcer.

faqs.question: Is green eye discharge contagious to other pets?
faqs.answer: Yes, many causes of green discharge, such as feline herpesvirus or calicivirus, are highly contagious to other cats in the household. It is best to isolate the affected cat until they have been seen by a vet.

faqs.question: How can I safely clean my cat's eye?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a clean, soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Use a fresh cloth for each eye to prevent spreading potential infection between them.
